old_claim.note: Supply Chain Management Software supports manufacturing, deployment, commercialization, or operational scaling.

new_claim.note: Warehouse barcode automation scales through supply-chain visibility and inventory-management software that turn scans into location, status, routing, and replenishment data.

source_shape.source_locator: GS1 US describes supply-chain visibility as using data and technology for real-time production, location, and status information, and says GS1 Standards enable automated inventory processes.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source links supply-chain visibility technology, real-time status data, GS1 standards, and automated inventory processes.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The edge is scaling rather than required because barcode scanning can exist locally, but warehouse-scale automation depends on software that manages scan-derived inventory and status data.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: supply_chain_management_software remains a commercial_or_scaling_dependency with stronger source support.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to standalone handheld barcode scanning with no inventory or supply-chain workflow integration.
A stronger source showed warehouse barcode automation should depend only on local database systems rather than supply-chain visibility software.
A more precise warehouse_management_systems prerequisite replaces the broader supply-chain software dependency.
short_reason: Barcode automation scales through software-managed inventory visibility.
